ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a mozzarella cheese food product class but no specific breaded-and-fried mozzarella stick entry; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated breaded mozzarella stick code; A00LC (processed/prepared cheese) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for breaded fried mozzarella sticks.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mozzarella cheese sticks consists of approximately 5 sticks weighing around 128 g, commonly served as a starter or snack with marinara dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Mozzarella Sticks, breaded, fried — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA FoodData Central for breaded, fried mozzarella sticks (NDB No. 21052 / FDC ID 168917). This is a direct match for the food as commercially prepared.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for breaded fried mozzarella sticks. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous breaded fried cheese snack foods and the GI of refined wheat breadcrumb coating (GI ~70) blended with the low-GI contribution of mozzarella cheese (GI ~0 for the cheese component). The composite GI of ~50 is consistent with similar breaded cheese products reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 29.95) / 100 ≈ 14.9, where carbohydrate per 128 g serving = 23.4 × 1.28 = 29.95 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (512 mg per 100 g; approximately 655 mg per standard serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mozzarella cheese stick consumption due to the high sodium content from both the cheese and seasoned breadcrumb coating, which can contribute significantly toward the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~14.9 per serving) from refined wheat breadcrumb coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mozzarella cheese sticks in moderation; the refined wheat breadcrumb coating contributes a moderate glycemic load per serving, and the high fat content may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (6.2 g per 100 g), high sodium (512 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying process
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit intake of mozzarella cheese sticks, as the combination of saturated fat from mozzarella, sodium from seasoning, and additional fat absorbed during deep-frying collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mozzarella cheese sticks are a popular Western snack and appetizer consisting of elongated pieces of mozzarella cheese that are coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and wheat flour batter, then deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior cheese melts into a soft, stretchy, gooey texture. Originating in the United States, they are widely served in casual dining restaurants, fast-food chains, and as a party snack, typically accompanied by marinara tomato dipping sauce. In Singapore, they appear on menus at Western-style cafes, burger joints, and fusion restaurants such as Riverside Buns at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, mozzarella cheese sticks provide moderate protein (12.5 g per 100 g) from the mozzarella cheese, a meaningful amount of calcium (290 mg per 100 g) supporting bone health, but are also high in sodium (512 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (6.2 g per 100 g) due to the cheese and frying process. The carbohydrate content (23.4 g per 100 g) comes primarily from the breadcrumb coating. Clinically, they are a concern for individuals managing hypertension due to high sodium, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and frying-derived trans fats. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low category), but the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 14.9, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. They are suitable for vegetarians but not vegans, and contain common allergens including milk, gluten, wheat, and eggs.
common_user_queries: How many calories are in mozzarella cheese sticks?
What is the GI of mozzarella cheese sticks?
Are mozzarella cheese sticks suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in fried cheese sticks?
What allergens are in mozzarella cheese sticks?
Can I eat mozzarella cheese sticks if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of mozzarella sticks?
Are cheese sticks high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central for breaded fried mozzarella sticks, which is a direct category match. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate is defensible based on comparable breaded cheese snack products. The cuisine tag in the input was listed as Taiwanese, which is not supported — mozzarella cheese sticks are a Western/American food served at a Taiwanese-style restaurant in Singapore; cuisine corrected to American/Western. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the GI estimation.
notes: 1. Input cuisine tag was 'Taiwanese' — this has been corrected to 'American / Western' as mozzarella cheese sticks are an American-origin food. The restaurant (Riverside Buns - Boat Quay) appears to serve a fusion/Western menu despite Taiwanese cuisine tags. 2. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 3. GI is an analogous estimate at 50 (low category, ≤55); no direct GI measurement found in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific product. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; mozzarella cheese sticks contain negligible vitamin C. 5. Halal status is marked false as the frying oil and breadcrumb seasoning sources are not confirmed halal-certified in the restaurant context. 6. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with actual USDA-sourced values where available. No -1 sentinel values remain in this record. 7. GL per serving = (50 × 29.95) / 100 = 14.975 ≈ 14.9, based on 128 g serving size and 23.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
